assertions.text: Null input is represented as a blank cell in the valid inputs @TableTest table (not the string 'null', and not as a separate @Test method). The blank cell maps to a null parameter.
assertions.evidence: Null input     |       |  — blank Input and Money? cells, confirmed by note: 'Null input goes in the valid table with a blank Input cell and a blank Money? cell — both map to null'

assertions.text: The exception/invalid-input table has an expected column (e.g. 'Throws?' or 'Exception?') specifying the exception type per row — rather than hardcoding the exception type in @Description or the method name.
assertions.evidence: Scenario        | Input  | Exception? — column 'Exception?' present with per-row values 'java.lang.IllegalArgumentException'

assertions.text: The exception cases (empty string, letters-only, negative) are handled — via a Throws? column, assertThrows in the method body, or a separate @TableTest — not silently omitted
assertions.evidence: Empty string | '' | java.lang.IllegalArgumentException; Letters only | abc | java.lang.IllegalArgumentException; Negative value | -5.00 | java.lang.IllegalArgumentException — all three cases present, assertThrows in method body

assertions.text: Valid inputs have a result column name ending with '?' (e.g. 'Money?' or 'Result?')
assertions.evidence: Scenario       | Input | Money? — column header 'Money?' in the valid-inputs table

assertions.text: Test method body contains no if or switch statements
assertions.evidence: parse_valid_and_null body: assertEquals(expected, parse(input)); parse_invalid_inputs_throw body: assertThrows(expectedException, () -> parse(input)); — neither contains if or switch

assertions.text: Test method has either a @DisplayName annotation or a method name that reads as a clear, descriptive title when converted from camelCase/snake_case (e.g. 'moneyParsing' → 'Money Parsing'). Not a generic name like 'test1' or 'testMethod'.
assertions.evidence: Method names 'parse_valid_and_null' and 'parse_invalid_inputs_throw' are clearly descriptive

assertions.text: @Description is present and provides context beyond what the table rows already express — such as the expected currency format, or open questions. Does NOT merely restate the column names or summarise what the rows show.
assertions.evidence: @Description("Valid string inputs produce a Money with the same value; null input returns null") and @Description("Empty string, non-numeric input, and negative values are rejected") — both merely summarise what the rows already show; no currency format details, no open questions, no context beyond the row data

assertions.text: Annotations appear in order: @DisplayName (if present), @Description (if present), @TableTest — not any other order.
assertions.evidence: @Description(...) appears immediately before @TableTest(...) in both methods; no @DisplayName present — correct order

assertions.text: @Description text uses a text block (triple-quoted string """) when the text is long, not string concatenation with +.
assertions.evidence: Both @Description values are short single sentences ('Valid string inputs produce a Money with the same value; null input returns null' and 'Empty string, non-numeric input, and negative values are rejected') — neither is long, so the text-block requirement does not apply; no string concatenation with + is used
